title: Stevens-Johnson Syndrome / TEN
oneLiner: Emergency, often drug-induced, epidermal detachment with mucosal involvement; rapid progression and high mortality mandate immediate recognition and cessation of culprit drug.

sections.Stevens-Johnson Syndrome / Toxic Epiderm: **One-liner:** Emergency, often drug-induced, epidermal detachment with mucosal involvement; rapid progression and high mortality mandate immediate recognition and cessation of culprit drug.
sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Airway compromise:** Stridor, hoarse voice, drooling – indicates laryngeal or tracheal mucosal sloughing. *Single finding: audible stridor or inability to manage secretions.*
- **Septic shock / multi-organ failure:** Widespread skin barrier loss leads to massive fluid loss and secondary infection. *Single finding: hypotension + tachycardia out of proportion to apparent skin detachment (≥10% BSA).*
- **Rapid progression of epidermal detachment:** Hourly extension of Nikolsky sign (gentle lateral pressure shears off epidermis) – >30% BSA in <24 h predicts very high mortality.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Prodrome: fever, malaise, sore throat, cough (1–3 days before rash) – often mistaken for viral illness.
  - Rash: starts as ill-defined, dusky macules with purpuric centres, often on trunk and face; becomes target-like (atypical targets with only 2 zones, no central blister – different from erythema multiforme).
  - Mucosal involvement (≥2 sites): painful, erosive stomatitis, conjunctivitis, urethritis, genital/anal ulceration.
  - Nikolsky sign positive: gentle lateral pressure causes separation of epidermis from dermis.
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Epidermal detachment: estimate BSA using "rule of nine" or palm method (patient's palm = 1% BSA).
  - Mucosal erosions: lip crusting, haemorrhagic crusts, pseudomembranes in mouth/eyes/genitalia.
  - Skin tenderness: often out of proportion to visible rash.
  - Absence of typical "target" lesions (seen in erythema multiforme major – better prognosis).
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Erythema multiforme major (EMM):**  
  - Symmetrical, typical target lesions (3 zones), acral distribution (hands, feet), mucosal involvement ≤2 sites.  
  - *Splitter:* Typical targets (3 zones) vs atypical targets (2 zones) – SJS/TEN has atypical targets + positive Nikolsky.
- **Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome (SSSS):**  
  - Widespread superficial erythema and desquamation, but no mucosal involvement; Nikolsky positive but only superficial splays (stratum corneum).  
  - *Splitter:* Check mucosal surfaces – SSSS always spares mucosae.
- **Drug-induced maculopapular rash:**  
  - Morbilliform, no epidermal detachment, no Nikolsky sign, no mucosal erosions.  
  - *Splitter:* Try rubbing a lesion – if it peels, it's SJS/TEN.
- **Acute generalised exanthematous pustulosis (AGEP):**  
  - Numerous sterile pustules on erythematous base, fever, neutrophilia – but no widespread epidermal necrosis.
- **Paraneoplastic pemphigus / autoimmune blistering:**  
  - More chronic, usually positive immunofluorescence, no acute fever.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - **Estimate BSA of epidermal detachment** (use diagram / patient's palm).
  - Check Nikolsky sign on a separate intact area.
  - **Urgent ophthalmology review** – slit-lamp examination for conjunctival pseudo-membrane, corneal ulceration.
- **Bloods:**
  - **FBC, U&E, CRP, LFTs, clotting** – look for leucopenia (poor prognosis), acute kidney injury, transaminitis.
  - **Blood cultures** × 2 (if febrile) – to guide antibiotics if sepsis.
  - **Skin biopsy** (punch or incisional) from a fresh blister or erythematous area – send for histology (full-thickness epidermal necrosis) and direct immunofluorescence (to rule out autoimmune blistering).
- **Imaging:**
  - **Chest X-ray** if respiratory symptoms – look for pneumothorax, pneumomediastinum (rare but serious) or ARDS.
  - CT chest if stridor/suspected airway involvement.
- **Special tests:**
  - **SCORTEN score** (prognostic – see Prognosis section) – calculate within 24 hours of admission.
  - **Drug causality assessment:** Take detailed drug history (all drugs started within 4 weeks, especially anticonvulsants, allopurinol, sulfonamides, NSAIDs, antiretroviral, antibiotics). Use ALDEN algorithm if needed.
- **Never-miss test:** **Skin biopsy** – failing to differentiate from SSSS or autoimmune bullous disease leads to wrong management and missed culprits.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Stop all suspect drugs immediately** – do NOT wait for confirmation.
  - **Secure airway** if any sign of respiratory distress (stridor, hoarseness, tachypnoea) – consult anaesthetics/critical care for intubation.
  - **Fluid resuscitation:** crystalloid (e.g. Hartmann's) 2–4 mL/kg per % BSA detachment in first 24 hours (similar to burns resuscitation) – but watch for fluid overload; titrate to urine output >0.5 mL/kg/h.
  - **Analgesia:** IV opioids (morphine) as needed for painful skin.
  - **Topical wound care:** non-adherent dressings (e.g. silicone or paraffin gauze); avoid adhesive tapes, unnecessary IV lines.
  - **Ophthalmology consult** – order preservative-free artificial tears and lubricating ointment; consider topical antibiotics if erosions.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - **Admit to HDU / ICU** if BSA >10% detachment or concerning airway.
  - **Multidisciplinary team:** dermatology, ophthalmology, critical care, burn surgeon, clinical pharmacist.
  - **Skin care:** aseptic handling; avoid barrier creams; use air-fluidised beds if available.
  - **Nutritional support:** consider NG feeding early – high protein/calorie intake.
  - **Prophylaxis for infection:** no routine prophylactic antibiotics – monitor for sepsis and treat if suspected.
  - **Mucosal care:** mouthwashes (e.g. chlorhexidine 0.2% 4 times per day), topical anaesthetic gels, vaginal lubricant.
  - **IVIG / cyclosporine / corticosteroids – uncertain benefit; [check local specialist protocol].** In UK, consensus favours supportive care only; some units use cyclosporine (3–5 mg/kg/day) if started early.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Ophthalmology follow-up** for chronic dry eye, symblepharon, entropion.
  - **Skin rehabilitation:** sun protection, avoid triggers, consider long-term psychological support.
  - **Drug rechallenge:** absolutely contraindicated; document allergy prominently in case notes and patient's electronic record.
- **Default Orders:**
  - **IV fluids:** Hartmann's at 125 mL/h (or burn formula adjusted – [check local policy]).
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** LMWH (e.g. enoxaparin 40 mg SC once daily) if low bleeding risk (avoid if extensive skin denudation or low platelets).
  - **Diet:** soft/liquid diet if oral mucosal pain; NBM if intubated.
  - **Observations:** hourly temperature, BP, HR, urine output; daily weight; skin mapping BSA every 12 h.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Analgesia:**
  - Morphine IV 2.5–5 mg as needed (or PCA) – avoid IM (risk of bleeding, skin trauma).
  - Paracetamol IV 1 g QDS (if no hepatic impairment).
- **Topical wound care:** Emulsifying ointment (e.g. 50% liquid paraffin in white soft paraffin) – apply generously. Avoid iodine or silver sulfadiazine (may worsen skin).
- **Ophthalmic:**
  - Chloramphenicol eye ointment 4 times per day (if purulent discharge).
  - Preservative-free artificial tears hourly.
- **IVIG:** 1–3 g/kg total over 2–4 days – dose varies; [check local policy].
- **Cyclosporine:** 3–5 mg/kg/day PO or IV in divided doses – [check local policy] for monitoring (blood pressure, renal function, trough levels).
- **Key contraindications/interactions:** Avoid corticosteroids in established SJS/TEN (increase infection risk, no proven benefit). Avoid all suspect drugs.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Cyclosporine – reduce dose in renal impairment; monitor levels. IVIG – caution in hyperviscosity.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Prescribing IV corticosteroids (e.g. high-dose methylprednisolone) as first-line** – worsens prognosis, delays wound healing, increases infection risk. Only consider under specialist guidance after ruling out EMM.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - **Airway obstruction** from mucosal sloughing (need tracheostomy).
  - **Sepsis / septic shock** (most common cause of death).
  - **Multi-organ failure** (renal, hepatic, respiratory).
  - **Gastrointestinal bleeding** from oesophageal or gastric mucosal involvement.
- **Chronic:**
  - **Ocular complications:** dry eye, conjunctival scarring, entropion, blindness (up to 50%).
  - **Skin:** permanent pigment change, nail loss, scarring, contractures.
  - **Vaginal/penile synechiae** – gynaecology/urology follow-up.
  - **Post-traumatic stress disorder** – psychological referral.
sections.Prognosis:
- **Mortality:** SCORTEN score predicts in-hospital death: 0–1 = 3%, 2 = 12%, 3 = 35%, 4 = 58%, ≥5 = 90%.  
- **Recovery:** survivors re-epithelialise in 2–4 weeks; chronic ocular and psychological morbidity common.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Estimate BSA of epidermal detachment accurately** – this drives triage to ICU and SCORTEN calculation. Use a body diagram.
- **Take a meticulous drug history** – list all medications started in past 4–8 weeks, including OTC, herbal, and intermittent use. This is your only chance to identify the culprit.
- **Get ophthalmology review within 2 hours** – early eye care reduces long-term visual loss.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do NOT prescribe corticosteroids** – they increase mortality in SJS/TEN; only use in EMM (if sure).  
- **Do NOT delay transfer to ICU/burns unit** – once BSA detachment >10% or airway signs, immediate escalation.  
- **Do NOT apply silver sulfadiazine** – it can worsen epidermal necrosis; use plain paraffin or silicon dressings.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**  
  - New blisters, worsening pain, new mucosal involvement.  
  - Fever, rigors, hypotension (sepsis).  
  - Difficulty swallowing or vision changes.
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Dermatology clinic in 4 weeks for skin surveillance.  
  - Ophthalmology at 1 month and then annually.  
  - GP for psychological support referral.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (dermatology / ICU registrar) immediately if:**  
  - BSA detachment >10% (admit to HDU/ICU).  
  - Any stridor, hoarse voice, or respiratory distress (call anaesthetics/critical care for airway assessment).  
  - New hypotension not responsive to 500 mL fluid bolus.  
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or lactate >2 mmol/L.  
  - SCORTEN ≥3 (mortality >35%) – discuss with ICU consultant.  
- **Crash team:** Cardiac arrest / severe airway obstruction.

rawMarkdown:
## Stevens-Johnson Syndrome / Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis (SJS/TEN)
**One-liner:** Emergency, often drug-induced, epidermal detachment with mucosal involvement; rapid progression and high mortality mandate immediate recognition and cessation of culprit drug.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Airway compromise:** Stridor, hoarse voice, drooling – indicates laryngeal or tracheal mucosal sloughing. *Single finding: audible stridor or inability to manage secretions.*
- **Septic shock / multi-organ failure:** Widespread skin barrier loss leads to massive fluid loss and secondary infection. *Single finding: hypotension + tachycardia out of proportion to apparent skin detachment (≥10% BSA).*
- **Rapid progression of epidermal detachment:** Hourly extension of Nikolsky sign (gentle lateral pressure shears off epidermis) – >30% BSA in <24 h predicts very high mortality.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Prodrome: fever, malaise, sore throat, cough (1–3 days before rash) – often mistaken for viral illness.
  - Rash: starts as ill-defined, dusky macules with purpuric centres, often on trunk and face; becomes target-like (atypical targets with only 2 zones, no central blister – different from erythema multiforme).
  - Mucosal involvement (≥2 sites): painful, erosive stomatitis, conjunctivitis, urethritis, genital/anal ulceration.
  - Nikolsky sign positive: gentle lateral pressure causes separation of epidermis from dermis.
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Epidermal detachment: estimate BSA using "rule of nine" or palm method (patient's palm = 1% BSA).
  - Mucosal erosions: lip crusting, haemorrhagic crusts, pseudomembranes in mouth/eyes/genitalia.
  - Skin tenderness: often out of proportion to visible rash.
  - Absence of typical "target" lesions (seen in erythema multiforme major – better prognosis).

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Erythema multiforme major (EMM):**  
  - Symmetrical, typical target lesions (3 zones), acral distribution (hands, feet), mucosal involvement ≤2 sites.  
  - *Splitter:* Typical targets (3 zones) vs atypical targets (2 zones) – SJS/TEN has atypical targets + positive Nikolsky.
- **Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome (SSSS):**  
  - Widespread superficial erythema and desquamation, but no mucosal involvement; Nikolsky positive but only superficial splays (stratum corneum).  
  - *Splitter:* Check mucosal surfaces – SSSS always spares mucosae.
- **Drug-induced maculopapular rash:**  
  - Morbilliform, no epidermal detachment, no Nikolsky sign, no mucosal erosions.  
  - *Splitter:* Try rubbing a lesion – if it peels, it's SJS/TEN.
- **Acute generalised exanthematous pustulosis (AGEP):**  
  - Numerous sterile pustules on erythematous base, fever, neutrophilia – but no widespread epidermal necrosis.
- **Paraneoplastic pemphigus / autoimmune blistering:**  
  - More chronic, usually positive immunofluorescence, no acute fever.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - **Estimate BSA of epidermal detachment** (use diagram / patient's palm).
  - Check Nikolsky sign on a separate intact area.
  - **Urgent ophthalmology review** – slit-lamp examination for conjunctival pseudo-membrane, corneal ulceration.
- **Bloods:**
  - **FBC, U&E, CRP, LFTs, clotting** – look for leucopenia (poor prognosis), acute kidney injury, transaminitis.
  - **Blood cultures** × 2 (if febrile) – to guide antibiotics if sepsis.
  - **Skin biopsy** (punch or incisional) from a fresh blister or erythematous area – send for histology (full-thickness epidermal necrosis) and direct immunofluorescence (to rule out autoimmune blistering).
- **Imaging:**
  - **Chest X-ray** if respiratory symptoms – look for pneumothorax, pneumomediastinum (rare but serious) or ARDS.
  - CT chest if stridor/suspected airway involvement.
- **Special tests:**
  - **SCORTEN score** (prognostic – see Prognosis section) – calculate within 24 hours of admission.
  - **Drug causality assessment:** Take detailed drug history (all drugs started within 4 weeks, especially anticonvulsants, allopurinol, sulfonamides, NSAIDs, antiretroviral, antibiotics). Use ALDEN algorithm if needed.
- **Never-miss test:** **Skin biopsy** – failing to differentiate from SSSS or autoimmune bullous disease leads to wrong management and missed culprits.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Stop all suspect drugs immediately** – do NOT wait for confirmation.
  - **Secure airway** if any sign of respiratory distress (stridor, hoarseness, tachypnoea) – consult anaesthetics/critical care for intubation.
  - **Fluid resuscitation:** crystalloid (e.g. Hartmann's) 2–4 mL/kg per % BSA detachment in first 24 hours (similar to burns resuscitation) – but watch for fluid overload; titrate to urine output >0.5 mL/kg/h.
  - **Analgesia:** IV opioids (morphine) as needed for painful skin.
  - **Topical wound care:** non-adherent dressings (e.g. silicone or paraffin gauze); avoid adhesive tapes, unnecessary IV lines.
  - **Ophthalmology consult** – order preservative-free artificial tears and lubricating ointment; consider topical antibiotics if erosions.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - **Admit to HDU / ICU** if BSA >10% detachment or concerning airway.
  - **Multidisciplinary team:** dermatology, ophthalmology, critical care, burn surgeon, clinical pharmacist.
  - **Skin care:** aseptic handling; avoid barrier creams; use air-fluidised beds if available.
  - **Nutritional support:** consider NG feeding early – high protein/calorie intake.
  - **Prophylaxis for infection:** no routine prophylactic antibiotics – monitor for sepsis and treat if suspected.
  - **Mucosal care:** mouthwashes (e.g. chlorhexidine 0.2% 4 times per day), topical anaesthetic gels, vaginal lubricant.
  - **IVIG / cyclosporine / corticosteroids – uncertain benefit; [check local specialist protocol].** In UK, consensus favours supportive care only; some units use cyclosporine (3–5 mg/kg/day) if started early.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Ophthalmology follow-up** for chronic dry eye, symblepharon, entropion.
  - **Skin rehabilitation:** sun protection, avoid triggers, consider long-term psychological support.
  - **Drug rechallenge:** absolutely contraindicated; document allergy prominently in case notes and patient's electronic record.
- **Default Orders:**
  - **IV fluids:** Hartmann's at 125 mL/h (or burn formula adjusted – [check local policy]).
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** LMWH (e.g. enoxaparin 40 mg SC once daily) if low bleeding risk (avoid if extensive skin denudation or low platelets).
  - **Diet:** soft/liquid diet if oral mucosal pain; NBM if intubated.
  - **Observations:** hourly temperature, BP, HR, urine output; daily weight; skin mapping BSA every 12 h.

### Drugs & Doses
- **Analgesia:**
  - Morphine IV 2.5–5 mg as needed (or PCA) – avoid IM (risk of bleeding, skin trauma).
  - Paracetamol IV 1 g QDS (if no hepatic impairment).
- **Topical wound care:** Emulsifying ointment (e.g. 50% liquid paraffin in white soft paraffin) – apply generously. Avoid iodine or silver sulfadiazine (may worsen skin).
- **Ophthalmic:**
  - Chloramphenicol eye ointment 4 times per day (if purulent discharge).
  - Preservative-free artificial tears hourly.
- **IVIG:** 1–3 g/kg total over 2–4 days – dose varies; [check local policy].
- **Cyclosporine:** 3–5 mg/kg/day PO or IV in divided doses – [check local policy] for monitoring (blood pressure, renal function, trough levels).
- **Key contraindications/interactions:** Avoid corticosteroids in established SJS/TEN (increase infection risk, no proven benefit). Avoid all suspect drugs.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Cyclosporine – reduce dose in renal impairment; monitor levels. IVIG – caution in hyperviscosity.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Prescribing IV corticosteroids (e.g. high-dose methylprednisolone) as first-line** – worsens prognosis, delays wound healing, increases infection risk. Only consider under specialist guidance after ruling out EMM.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - **Airway obstruction** from mucosal sloughing (need tracheostomy).
  - **Sepsis / septic shock** (most common cause of death).
  - **Multi-organ failure** (renal, hepatic, respiratory).
  - **Gastrointestinal bleeding** from oesophageal or gastric mucosal involvement.
- **Chronic:**
  - **Ocular complications:** dry eye, conjunctival scarring, entropion, blindness (up to 50%).
  - **Skin:** permanent pigment change, nail loss, scarring, contractures.
  - **Vaginal/penile synechiae** – gynaecology/urology follow-up.
  - **Post-traumatic stress disorder** – psychological referral.

### Prognosis
- **Mortality:** SCORTEN score predicts in-hospital death: 0–1 = 3%, 2 = 12%, 3 = 35%, 4 = 58%, ≥5 = 90%.  
- **Recovery:** survivors re-epithelialise in 2–4 weeks; chronic ocular and psychological morbidity common.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Estimate BSA of epidermal detachment accurately** – this drives triage to ICU and SCORTEN calculation. Use a body diagram.
- **Take a meticulous drug history** – list all medications started in past 4–8 weeks, including OTC, herbal, and intermittent use. This is your only chance to identify the culprit.
- **Get ophthalmology review within 2 hours** – early eye care reduces long-term visual loss.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do NOT prescribe corticosteroids** – they increase mortality in SJS/TEN; only use in EMM (if sure).  
- **Do NOT delay transfer to ICU/burns unit** – once BSA detachment >10% or airway signs, immediate escalation.  
- **Do NOT apply silver sulfadiazine** – it can worsen epidermal necrosis; use plain paraffin or silicon dressings.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**  
  - New blisters, worsening pain, new mucosal involvement.  
  - Fever, rigors, hypotension (sepsis).  
  - Difficulty swallowing or vision changes.
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Dermatology clinic in 4 weeks for skin surveillance.  
  - Ophthalmology at 1 month and then annually.  
  - GP for psychological support referral.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (dermatology / ICU registrar) immediately if:**  
  - BSA detachment >10% (admit to HDU/ICU).  
  - Any stridor, hoarse voice, or respiratory distress (call anaesthetics/critical care for airway assessment).  
  - New hypotension not responsive to 500 mL fluid bolus.  
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or lactate >2 mmol/L.  
  - SCORTEN ≥3 (mortality >35%) – discuss with ICU consultant.  
- **Crash team:** Cardiac arrest / severe airway obstruction.
